M2 · The Efron log-spline prior

Source: vignettes/m2-efron-log-spline-prior.Rmd
m2-efron-log-spline-prior.Rmd

Scope of this vignette

This is the second vignette in the methodological track. M1 established the deconvolution setting and motivated the log-spline family as the prior on the mixing distribution gg. M2 specifies that prior. The goal is a complete account of the parameterisation that the package’s Stan source implements: the continuous form on ℝ\mathbb{R}, the discretisation onto the grid, the softmax normalisation that yields a proper simplex, the identifiability convention that fixes the intercept-induced ridge, the Gaussian conditional prior on the spline coefficients, the half-Cauchy hyperprior on the smoothness precision, and the visual character of the family under varying precision.

Notation reminder

M1 introduced the symbols. They recur unchanged: KK is the number of sites; θi\theta_i is the latent effect at site ii and θ̂i\hat\theta_i its observed estimate; σi\sigma_i is the known within-study standard error; gg is the mixing distribution to be recovered; LL is the grid length; MM is the spline dimension; α∈ℝM\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^M is the spline-coefficient vector; and λ\lambda is the smoothness precision that governs the Gaussian prior on α\alpha. No new symbols are introduced in this vignette except auxiliary intermediates used in the derivations.

The continuous form

On the real line, the log-spline prior writes the unnormalised log-density of gg at θ\theta as a linear function of α\alpha: log⁡g̃α(θ)=B(θ)⊤α,gα(θ)∝exp⁡(B(θ)⊤α), \log \tilde g_{\alpha}(\theta) \;=\; B(\theta)^{\top} \alpha, \qquad g_{\alpha}(\theta) \;\propto\; \exp\!\big(B(\theta)^{\top}\alpha\big), where B(θ)=(B1(θ),…,BM(θ))⊤B(\theta) = (B_1(\theta), \ldots, B_M(\theta))^{\top} is the length-MM natural-cubic-spline basis vector evaluated at θ\theta. The basis functions Bk(⋅)B_k(\cdot) are fixed by choosing knot locations and the degree of the polynomial pieces; only α\alpha is a parameter.

The exponential parameterisation is not a cosmetic choice. Three of its properties shape every downstream computation in the package.

First, the map α↦gα\alpha \mapsto g_{\alpha} is unconstrained on ℝM\mathbb{R}^M. Replacing the linear form B(θ)⊤αB(\theta)^{\top}\alpha with gα(θ)g_{\alpha}(\theta) directly would require sign constraints to keep gg non-negative, which is awkward both analytically and algorithmically. The exp⁡(⋅)\exp(\,\cdot\,) wrapper enforces positivity identically in α\alpha, so α\alpha can range freely over ℝM\mathbb{R}^M without boundary handling.

Second, the family {gα:α∈ℝM}\{g_{\alpha} : \alpha \in \mathbb{R}^M\} is an exponential family with natural parameter α\alpha and sufficient statistic B(θ)B(\theta). Its log-density is linear in α\alpha, and the log-partition function ϕ(α)=log⁡∫exp⁡(B(θ)⊤α)dθ \phi(\alpha) \;=\; \log \int \exp\!\big(B(\theta)^{\top}\alpha\big)\, \mathrm{d}\theta is convex on ℝM\mathbb{R}^M (this is a standard consequence of Hölder’s inequality and is recorded in the package’s derivation appendix). Convexity of ϕ\phi supplies stable derivatives for the normalised log-spline density. HMC performance on the α\alpha block at fixed λ\lambda is therefore an empirical diagnostic question, not a consequence of global log-concavity: the marginal likelihood under the deconvolution mixture is a log of a weighted Gaussian mixture, which is not generally log-concave in α\alpha.

Third, the gradient of the log-density with respect to α\alpha is just B(θ)−∇ϕ(α)B(\theta) - \nabla\phi(\alpha), and ∇ϕ(α)\nabla\phi(\alpha) is the expectation of B(Θ)B(\Theta) under gαg_{\alpha}. Reverse-mode autodiff in Stan computes this gradient at every leapfrog step in a single matrix-vector multiplication of cost O(LM)O(LM).

Discretisation onto a grid

bayesEfron does not work with the continuous form directly. Instead the package fixes a length-LL grid {θ1,…,θL}⊂ℝ\{\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_L\} \subset \mathbb{R} chosen before sampling, replaces the integral in ϕ\phi with a sum, and treats gg as a probability mass on the grid. The discrete prior is gl(α)=exp⁡(Bl⊤α)∑j=1Lexp⁡(Bj⊤α),l=1,…,L, g_l(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\big(B_l^{\top}\alpha\big)} {\sum_{j=1}^{L} \exp\!\big(B_j^{\top}\alpha\big)}, \qquad l = 1, \ldots, L, where Bl∈ℝMB_l \in \mathbb{R}^M is the ll-th row of the basis matrix B∈ℝL×MB \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times M} evaluated at the grid: B=ns(θ1,…,θL;df=M,intercept=FALSE)B = \mathrm{ns}(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_L;\ \mathrm{df} = M,\ \mathrm{intercept} = \mathrm{FALSE}). The denominator is the finite-sum log-partition ϕ(α)=log⁡∑j=1Lexp⁡(Bj⊤α)=log_sum_exp(Bα), \phi(\alpha) \;=\; \log \sum_{j=1}^{L} \exp\!\big(B_j^{\top}\alpha\big) \;=\; \mathrm{log\_sum\_exp}(B\alpha), and the discrete prior is the softmax of BαB\alpha: g(α)=softmax(Bα)g(\alpha) = \mathrm{softmax}(B\alpha), lying in the simplex ΔL−1\Delta^{L-1} by construction.

Two properties of this discretisation are worth stating explicitly.

The normalisation is exact on the grid, not approximate. The continuous-form integral ∫exp⁡(B(θ)⊤α)dθ\int \exp(B(\theta)^{\top}\alpha)\, \mathrm{d}\theta would itself need to be approximated by a quadrature rule, but the discrete model has no integral to approximate: it replaces the continuum by the grid and normalises the mass on the grid exactly. The relation between the discrete prior and the continuous form is the usual Riemann-sum limit ∑j:θj≤tgj→∫−∞tg(θ)dθ\sum_{j:\theta_j \le t} g_j \to \int_{-\infty}^{t} g(\theta)\, \mathrm{d}\theta as the grid spacing tends to zero, but the inferential target is the discrete prior gg; vignette M4 discusses the grid-resolution trade-off in detail.

The Stan source computes log⁡g=log⁡softmax(Bα)=Bα−ϕ(α)𝟏L\log g = \log \mathrm{softmax}(B\alpha) = B\alpha - \phi(\alpha)\,\mathbf{1}_L via Stan’s built-in log_softmax, which is implemented using the max-shift identity to avoid overflow when BαB\alpha spans tens of orders of magnitude. The quantity log⁡g\log g is what enters the mixture log-likelihood (M3); the simplex g=exp⁡(log⁡g)g = \exp(\log g) is what enters the prior-summary generated quantities.

Identifiability of the spline coefficients

The softmax is invariant to a constant shift of its argument: softmax(Bα+c𝟏L)=softmax(Bα) \mathrm{softmax}(B\alpha + c\,\mathbf{1}_L) \;=\; \mathrm{softmax}(B\alpha) for any c∈ℝc \in \mathbb{R}. If the basis matrix BB has the constant vector 𝟏L\mathbf{1}_L in its column space — that is, if there exists v0∈ℝMv_0 \in \mathbb{R}^M with Bv0=𝟏LB v_0 = \mathbf{1}_L — then α\alpha and α+tv0\alpha + t v_0 produce identical gg for every tt, and the joint posterior is degenerate along that direction. The package handles this two ways.

The first is parameterisation. The basis matrix is constructed with splines::ns(grid, df = M, intercept = FALSE), where intercept = FALSE instructs splines::ns() to omit the constant column from the natural-cubic-spline basis. The resulting BB does not include the constant direction by construction, so the most direct form of the shift invariance is removed at the basis construction step.

The second is a runtime guard. Whether the columns of BB span the orthogonal complement of 𝟏L\mathbf{1}_L — and hence whether 𝟏L∉col(B)\mathbf{1}_L \notin \mathrm{col}(B) — is not an automatic algebraic consequence of intercept = FALSE for every admissible grid configuration. The package therefore enforces a rank postcondition in make_efron_grid(): the augmented matrix [𝟏LB][\mathbf{1}_L \;\;\; B] must have rank M+1M + 1. The check is qr(cbind(1, B), tol = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps))$rank == M + 1L. Failure raises the typed condition bef_grid_rank_deficient before any Stan compilation occurs. With the rank postcondition satisfied, α\alpha is identified on ℝM\mathbb{R}^M and the joint posterior on (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) is proper.

The smoothness hyperprior

The conditional prior on α\alpha is a centred Gaussian whose variance is parameterised by the inverse of λ\lambda: α∣λ∼𝒩(𝟎,λ−1𝐈M). \alpha \mid \lambda \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{0},\, \lambda^{-1}\,\mathbf{I}_M\right). The convention is variance-form: the second argument of 𝒩(μ,σ2)\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) is variance, so each component of α\alpha has variance 1/λ1/\lambda and standard deviation λ−1/2\lambda^{-1/2}. The Stan source declares the same prior in standard-deviation form as alpha ~ normal(0, inv_sqrt(lambda)), which Stan’s normal(mu, sd) interprets identically.

The role of λ\lambda is shrinkage on α\alpha. Larger λ\lambda concentrates α\alpha near zero, so BαB\alpha is small for every θ\theta, the softmax is close to uniform, and gg is smooth and flat. Smaller λ\lambda admits larger α\alpha, so BαB\alpha can vary substantially across the grid and the resulting gg can exhibit peaks, asymmetry, and modest multimodality.

Because λ\lambda controls the effective smoothness of gg but is itself unknown, the package places a hyperprior on it. The choice is the half-Cauchy: λ∼Cauchy+(0,5), \lambda \;\sim\; \mathrm{Cauchy}^{+}(0, 5), a half-Cauchy on λ\lambda with scale parameter 55. Two properties of the half-Cauchy commend it for this role, following the weakly-informative-prior tradition canonically framed by Gelman (2006, Bayesian Analysis).

The first is heavy tails. The half-Cauchy has no moments, so neither its mean nor its variance is defined; the implicit prior weight at large λ\lambda decays only as λ−2\lambda^{-2}. This is heavy enough that the data can override the prior when shape evidence is strong, pulling λ\lambda either upward (more smoothing) or downward (less smoothing) without resistance from prior-induced curvature. The heavy upper tail in particular means that the prior does not force λ\lambda to be small even when the data are uninformative; the prior admits “smooth” as a default consistent with weak data.

The second is mass at small λ\lambda. The half-Cauchy assigns non-negligible density to small positive values of λ\lambda, allowing the conditional Gaussian on α\alpha to be wide when the data warrant a wiggly gg. A half-normal alternative would have a lighter upper tail, placing less prior mass on very large λ\lambda than the half-Cauchy. Near zero, both priors can assign non-negligible density.

The combination of the proper conditional prior on α\alpha given λ\lambda and the proper hyperprior on λ\lambda is sufficient, together with the bounded mixture likelihood (M3), for a proper joint posterior on (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda). The package documents the proper-posterior conditions in its derivation appendix; the appropriate forward reference is M3.

The Cauchy citation here is to Gelman’s weakly-informative-prior rationale rather than to a specific prescription that the half-Cauchy belongs on a precision parameter — Gelman’s exposition canonically places the half-Cauchy on a scale parameter, e.g., a hierarchical-Gaussian standard deviation. The half-Cauchy on λ\lambda used here is paper-faithful to the published Lee & Sui (2025) implementation, and the bijection σα=λ−1/2\sigma_\alpha = \lambda^{-1/2} relates the two parameterisations.

A smoothness sweep

The visual character of the family {gα}\{g_{\alpha}\} under varying implicit smoothness is most easily appreciated by sampling α\alpha from the conditional prior at several values of λ\lambda and plotting the corresponding gg. The figure below shows three such draws on the package’s default paper_realdata grid. The standard deviations correspond to λ∈{1,4,100}\lambda \in \{1, 4, 100\} — high-variance (loose) at λ=1\lambda = 1, moderate at λ=4\lambda = 4, and tight at λ=100\lambda = 100.

set.seed(2026)

grid_obj <- make_efron_grid(
  theta_hat = seq(-1.0, 1.0, length.out = 9),
  sigma     = rep(0.15, 9),
  L         = 101L,
  M         = 6L
)
theta_grid <- grid_obj$grid
B          <- grid_obj$B
M          <- grid_obj$M

log_sum_exp <- function(x) {
  m <- max(x)
  m + log(sum(exp(x - m)))
}
g_alpha <- function(alpha) {
  log_w <- as.numeric(B %*% alpha)
  exp(log_w - log_sum_exp(log_w))
}

alpha_low  <- rnorm(M, sd = 1.0)   # lambda = 1
alpha_med  <- rnorm(M, sd = 0.5)   # lambda = 4
alpha_high <- rnorm(M, sd = 0.1)   # lambda = 100
g_low  <- g_alpha(alpha_low)
g_med  <- g_alpha(alpha_med)
g_high <- g_alpha(alpha_high)

if (has_ggplot2) {
  df_sweep <- rbind(
    data.frame(theta = theta_grid, mass = g_low,
               panel = "lambda = 1 (loose)"),
    data.frame(theta = theta_grid, mass = g_med,
               panel = "lambda = 4 (moderate)"),
    data.frame(theta = theta_grid, mass = g_high,
               panel = "lambda = 100 (tight)")
  )
  df_sweep$panel <- factor(
    df_sweep$panel,
    levels = c("lambda = 1 (loose)",
               "lambda = 4 (moderate)",
               "lambda = 100 (tight)")
  )
  ggplot2::ggplot(df_sweep,
                  ggplot2::aes(x = theta, y = mass)) +
    ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
    ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
    ggplot2::labs(
      x = expression(theta),
      y = expression(g[alpha](theta)),
      title = NULL
    ) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
} else {
  par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 1.5, 1))
  plot(theta_grid, g_low,  type = "l", lwd = 1,
       xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(g[alpha](theta)),
       main = "lambda = 1 (loose)")
  plot(theta_grid, g_med,  type = "l", lwd = 1,
       xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(g[alpha](theta)),
       main = "lambda = 4 (moderate)")
  plot(theta_grid, g_high, type = "l", lwd = 1,
       xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(g[alpha](theta)),
       main = "lambda = 100 (tight)")
}

Three log-spline priors g_alpha(theta) at decreasing alpha variance: low precision (lambda = 1, top), medium precision (lambda = 4, middle), and high precision (lambda = 100, bottom). Higher precision pulls the density toward the spline-uniform reference; lower precision admits multimodality.

The three panels exhibit the expected behaviour. At λ=100\lambda = 100 (bottom), α\alpha is tightly shrunk toward zero, BαB\alpha is small in magnitude, and the softmax sits close to the spline-uniform reference: the density is broad, gently sloped, and lacks distinct modes. At λ=4\lambda = 4 (middle), some shape structure emerges but the density remains unimodal or nearly so. At λ=1\lambda = 1 (top), α\alpha has substantial dispersion, BαB\alpha varies markedly across the grid, and the resulting gg shows asymmetry and multimodality. The contrast across the panels is what motivates the data-driven choice of λ\lambda: the family is rich enough to express both near-uniform and visibly multimodal priors, and the half-Cauchy hyperprior lets the marginal likelihood select among them rather than fixing a single smoothness level by hand.

The four-level Bayesian hierarchy

Composing the components specified above yields the full hierarchical model on KK sites. Reading from the data upward: θ̂i∣θi∼𝒩(θi,σi2),i=1,…,K,θi∣g∼iidg,gα(θl)=exp⁡(Bl⊤α)∑j=1Lexp⁡(Bj⊤α),l=1,…,L,α∣λ∼𝒩(𝟎,λ−1𝐈M),λ∼Cauchy+(0,5). \begin{aligned} \hat\theta_i \mid \theta_i &\;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta_i, \sigma_i^2\right), \qquad i = 1, \ldots, K, \\ \theta_i \mid g &\;\stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\; g, \\ g_{\alpha}(\theta_l) &\;=\; \frac{\exp\!\big(B_l^{\top}\alpha\big)} {\sum_{j=1}^{L} \exp\!\big(B_j^{\top}\alpha\big)}, \qquad l = 1, \ldots, L, \\ \alpha \mid \lambda &\;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mathbf{0},\, \lambda^{-1}\,\mathbf{I}_M\right), \\ \lambda &\;\sim\; \mathrm{Cauchy}^{+}(0, 5). \end{aligned} The four levels are: the sampling model that relates the observed estimates to the latent effects (level 1); the categorical draw of the latent effects from the discrete prior gg on the grid (level 2); the log-spline parameterisation of gg in terms of the spline coefficients α\alpha (level 3); and the smoothness hyperprior on λ\lambda that regularises α\alpha (level 4).

The latent θi\theta_i are integrated out of the joint analytically: the per-site marginal likelihood is the finite mixture p(θ̂i∣g,σi)=∑j=1Lgj𝒩(θ̂i;θj,σi2)p(\hat\theta_i \mid g, \sigma_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{L} g_j\, \mathcal{N}(\hat\theta_i;\, \theta_j, \sigma_i^2), and the sampler runs over the unconstrained pair (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) in dimension M+1M + 1 (default 77) regardless of KK. Vignette M3 unpacks the hierarchy level by level, derives the marginal likelihood, and characterises the small-KK prior-dominance regime that follows from the heavy-tailed hyperprior on λ\lambda.

Computational structure for HMC

The log-posterior of (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda) under the hierarchy above is everywhere differentiable. The log-spline parameterisation contributes a term linear in α\alpha (through BαB\alpha) plus the log-partition ϕ(α)\phi(\alpha), whose gradient is ∇ϕ(α)=∑jgj(α)Bj\nabla\phi(\alpha) = \sum_j g_j(\alpha)\, B_j and whose Hessian is the covariance matrix of BJB_J under gαg_{\alpha} — both available in closed form. The conditional prior on α\alpha contributes a quadratic-in-α\alpha term, and the half-Cauchy on λ\lambda contributes a smooth log⁡(1+λ2/25)\log(1 + \lambda^2/25) term. Combined with the mixture log-sum-exp of the likelihood (M3), the entire target is twice continuously differentiable in (α,λ)(\alpha, \lambda), and reverse-mode autodiff in Stan produces the gradient at cost O(KL+LM)O(KL + LM) per leapfrog step.

This differentiability is the algorithmic reason the log-spline prior is HMC-friendly, and it is the reason bayesEfron is implemented in Stan rather than in a Metropolis-style sampler. The Stan source inst/stan/efron_re.stan exploits the structure via Stan’s log_softmax, log_sum_exp, and normal primitives, all of which are differentiable through reverse-mode autodiff. Vignette M5 walks through the Stan source line by line and documents the ten numerical-stability disciplines that the source obeys.

Reading map

This vignette specified the prior. The remaining methodological vignettes carry the construction into implementation and verification.

  • M3 — The random-effects hierarchy. Unpacks the four levels, derives the per-site mixture likelihood, characterises the per-site posterior weights via the max-shift trick, distinguishes theta_mean (estimator) from theta_rep (parameter draw), and states the proper-posterior conditions.
  • M4 — Grid construction. Documents the four grid recipes exposed by make_efron_grid() — paper_realdata, paper_simulation, paper_sensitivity, and the experimental KL-target recipe — and the trade-offs between grid width and resolution.
  • M5 — Stan model and cache. The byte-locked Stan source, the numerical-stability disciplines (log_softmax, log_sum_exp, max-shift normalisation, two-pass variance), and the cache that prevents redundant compilation.
  • M6 — Verification and calibration. End-to-end coverage verification on the locked benchmark fixtures and the release-blocking acceptance band on the per-site replicate intervals.

A reader who has followed M1 and M2 has the complete model specification in hand; the remaining vignettes connect that specification to its computational realisation and its empirical calibration.

On this page

Developed by JoonHo Lee.
